jonnify·bcs /bcs/cache/the-lane-that-remembers/the-bus-dies-the-lane-replays
Transcript The memory The loss drill References

B4.4 · dive 2 — the bus dies, the lane replays

The bus dies; the lane replays.

D-2 keeps the bus volatile on purpose, so a bus restart erases queued coherence jobs — Chapter 4.2 stated that ceiling in the open, and the TTL was the floor under the loss. This dive raises the ceiling without touching the decision. The H3 gate proves the applied memory outlives everything around it — a replayed old version answers :remembered_stale from the journal alone, no cache row consulted, none created — and the H4 gate stages D-2's loss itself: fifty obligations on the lane, twenty applied, the queue flushed, and the journal replaying back exactly 30 uncovered intents … in seq order under their recorded job ids.

Source: content/bcs4.4.md, quoting bcs_rung_4_4_check.out. This dive reads the memory derive, H3, the loss derive, and H4 — the derived expectation: a remembered count of 50.

§1

The transcript

bcs_rung_4_4_check.out · verbatim · this dive reads the memory derive, H3, the loss derive, H4

Each derive line states the expectation before the gate asserts it: the H3 derive demands :remembered_stale with no cache involvement at all, and the H4 derive computes the replay set's size from the staging itself — 50 recorded, 20 applied, so exactly 30 uncovered.

§2

The memory across a restart

content/bcs4.4.md · What · Decisions

The applied table lives in the file, so it survives the table, the node, and the bus. Without this memory, an empty L1 makes every replayed message look fresh; with it, staleness is a fact about the lane, not about whichever rows happen to be resident. The drill stops the table and the journal, reopens the same file against an empty L1, and replays an old version — the gate's verbatim close: the table's verdict counters did not move and no row appeared -- and the genuinely newer v6 passed through and became the new last word.

The decision underneath: the memory check runs before the cache. apply_and_remember consults the journal first and answers :remembered_stale without an ETS lookup, because the journal's word outlives the cache's rows — the gate proves the counters do not move. This is newer-wins from B4.2 · Coherence by Mint Time given a durable floor: the comparison is the same, the version is still a name, and the only change is where the older side of the comparison survives.

§3

The loss drill — 50, 20, 30

content/bcs4.4.md · What · Who · Boundaries

D-2 keeps the bus volatile, so a bus restart erases queued coherence jobs. The rung stages exactly that — 50 intents recorded and enqueued, 20 applied before the loss, the lane's queue keys flushed — and replay selects by coverage: every intent whose name lacks an applied version at least as new. The twenty already-applied intents are not re-sent; the thirty lost ones are, in seq order, under their recorded job ids, with no acknowledgment bookkeeping anywhere on the hot path.

Recovery, after the bus came back empty, is one idempotent verb — and retention is its sibling, both safe to run on a schedule:

{:ok, %{replayed: n, deduplicated: m}} = Journal.replay(:limits_journal, conn)
{:ok, retired} = Journal.compact(:limits_journal)

Boundaries, stated honestly: the journal is per-group and per-node — it restores its own lane's obligations and claims nothing about cross-group ordering, which the lanes never promised anyway. Replay is at-least-once by construction and harmless by comparison — the same two facts, in the same order, as everywhere else in this part. The lease, the reaper, and the volatile substrate under this drill are B3.3 · The State Machine in Lua's machine; the recorded job ids the replay reuses ride B3.2 · Jobs Are Entities's admission dedup. Application on the consumer side is the engine of the sibling module — B4.3 · The Single Writer and the Ring — and the compaction predicate and the price close the module in the next dive.

§4

References

sources · related
